content.text: Support agents handle customer and employee inquiries in real time — answering product questions, looking up order status, issuing refunds within policy limits, and escalating to a human when the situation warrants it. Unlike back-office automation agents that execute batch workflows, support agents maintain multi-turn conversation state and must be empathetic, concise, and grounded in a knowledge base. Getting the escalation logic right — knowing exactly when to hand off — is as important as the resolution path.

attrs.alt: LLM support agent architecture: user channels (web chat, email, Slack, phone) flow into an agent core (intent classification → KB retrieval → account context → resolve or escalate), which dispatches to backend tools (kb_search, get_account, create_ticket, issue_refund, escalate_to_human). Bottom panels show system prompt design rules and key metrics.

content.text: Conversation State Management

Support conversations span multiple turns. Each message must be appended to the conversation history and persisted — if the session server restarts, the user must not lose context. Store history keyed by
conversation_id

in Redis (for fast reads) with a TTL of several hours. For compliance (GDPR, HIPAA), also write to a durable store. Trim history to stay within the model's context window — keep the last N turns plus the system prompt.

import json
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import timedelta
from typing import Literal

import redis


MessageRole = Literal["system", "user", "assistant", "tool"]


@dataclass
class ConversationStore:
    redis_client: redis.Redis
    ttl: timedelta = timedelta(hours=4)
    max_turns: int = 20  # keep last 20 turns to fit context window

    def _key(self, conversation_id: str) -> str:
        return f"support:conv:{conversation_id}"

    def load(self, conversation_id: str) -> list[dict]:
        raw = self.redis_client.get(self._key(conversation_id))
        return json.loads(raw) if raw else []

    def append(self, conversation_id: str, message: dict) -> list[dict]:
        history = self.load(conversation_id)
        history.append(message)
        # Keep system prompt (index 0) + last max_turns messages
        if len(history) > self.max_turns + 1:
            history = history[:1] + history[-(self.max_turns):]
        self.redis_client.setex(
            self._key(conversation_id),
            int(self.ttl.total_seconds()),
            json.dumps(history),
        )
        return history

    def close(self, conversation_id: str, outcome: str) -> None:
        """Mark conversation resolved; write to durable store for audit."""
        history = self.load(conversation_id)
        self.redis_client.delete(self._key(conversation_id))
        # Persist to DB (omitted): insert into support_conversations table
        _ = history, outcome  # placeholder
Tool Definitions and the Agent Loop

Support agent tools split into three categories: read-only lookup (KB search, account fetch), policy-gated writes (create ticket, issue refund up to a dollar limit), and the escalation gate. The LLM decides which tool to call based on the user's message and conversation history. After tool results are returned, it generates the customer-facing reply.
Knowledge Base Retrieval

The KB search tool retrieves articles from a vector store indexed on your documentation, FAQs, and past resolved tickets. Return snippet text plus the article URL so the LLM can cite its source in the answer. Citation grounding dramatically reduces hallucination: if the answer is not in the retrieved snippets, the model should say so rather than fabricate.

import asyncio
from dataclasses import dataclass

import httpx


@dataclass
class KBArticle:
    article_id: str
    title: str
    snippet: str   # 200-300 word excerpt most relevant to the query
    url: str
    score: float


async def kb_search(query: str, top_k: int = 5,
                    embed_fn, vector_db) -> list[dict]:
    """Embed query, search vector store, return snippets."""
    query_embedding = await embed_fn(query)
    results = await vector_db.query(
        vector=query_embedding,
        top_k=top_k,
        filter={"type": "kb_article"},  # exclude ticket history, etc.
        include_metadata=True,
    )
    articles = [
        {
            "title": r.metadata["title"],
            "snippet": r.metadata["snippet"],
            "url": r.metadata["url"],
            "relevance_score": round(r.score, 3),
        }
        for r in results.matches
    ]
    # Drop low-relevance results to avoid noise in context
    return [a for a in articles if a["relevance_score"] > 0.72]


def build_kb_index_entry(article: dict) -> dict:
    """Prepare a KB article for indexing."""
    # Chunk long articles at paragraph boundaries
    chunks = split_into_chunks(article["body"], max_tokens=300, overlap=30)
    return [
        {
            "id": f"{article['id']}::{i}",
            "text": chunk,
            "metadata": {
                "type": "kb_article",
                "article_id": article["id"],
                "title": article["title"],
                "url": article["url"],
                "snippet": chunk[:250],
                "updated_at": article["updated_at"],
            },
        }
        for i, chunk in enumerate(chunks)
    ]


def split_into_chunks(text: str, max_tokens: int, overlap: int) -> list[str]:
    # Simplified: split at paragraph boundaries
    paragraphs = [p.strip() for p in text.split("\n\n") if p.strip()]
    chunks, current, current_len = [], [], 0
    for para in paragraphs:
        length = len(para.split())
        if current_len + length > max_tokens and current:
            chunks.append(" ".join(current))
            current = current[-overlap:] if overlap else []
            current_len = sum(len(p.split()) for p in current)
        current.append(para)
        current_len += length
    if current:
        chunks.append(" ".join(current))
    return chunks
Escalation Logic
Escalation should be a first-class tool call, not a fallback error state. Include it in the tool list with a clear description of when to invoke it. When
escalate_to_human

is called, the implementation creates a ticket with the full transcript, a model-generated context summary, and a priority label. The human agent receives everything they need without having to re-read the conversation from scratch.

from enum import Enum


class EscalationPriority(str, Enum):
    LOW = "low"
    NORMAL = "normal"
    HIGH = "high"
    URGENT = "urgent"


def escalate_to_human(
    reason: str,
    priority: str,
    summary: str,
    conversation_id: str,
    user_id: str,
    transcript: list[dict],
    ticketing_client,
) -> dict:
    """Create a support ticket and route to a human queue."""
    ticket = ticketing_client.create_ticket(
        subject=f"[{priority.upper()}] Support escalation: {reason}",
        description=(
            f"Agent summary: {summary}\n\n"
            f"Conversation ID: {conversation_id}\n"
            f"User ID: {user_id}\n\n"
            "Full transcript:\n"
            + format_transcript(transcript)
        ),
        priority=priority,
        tags=["agent-escalation", f"reason:{reason.replace(' ', '-')}"],
        custom_fields={"conversation_id": conversation_id},
    )
    # Notify on-call support channel
    if priority in (EscalationPriority.HIGH, EscalationPriority.URGENT):
        ticketing_client.notify_channel(
            channel="#support-urgent",
            message=f"High-priority escalation: ticket #{ticket['id']} — {reason}",
        )
    return {
        "ticket_id": ticket["id"],
        "estimated_response": ticket["sla_due"],
        "message_to_user": (
            f"I've connected you with our support team (ticket #{ticket['id']}). "
            f"You'll hear back by {ticket['sla_due']}."
        ),
    }


def format_transcript(messages: list[dict]) -> str:
    lines = []
    for m in messages:
        role = m.get("role", "")
        if role == "system":
            continue
        content = m.get("content") or ""
        lines.append(f"{role.upper()}: {content}")
    return "\n".join(lines)
Sentiment Detection for Proactive Escalation

Rather than waiting for the agent to decide to escalate, you can run a lightweight sentiment check on every user message and proactively trigger escalation when frustration signals are detected. This is especially useful when users do not explicitly ask for a human but their language indicates high dissatisfaction.
Measuring Agent Effectiveness

The primary business metric is deflection rate — the percentage of sessions the agent resolves without human involvement. But deflection rate alone is misleading if users are deflected but not satisfied. Pair it with CSAT collected immediately after session close. Track these metrics per intent category, since some topics (billing disputes, legal requests) should always escalate and inflating their deflection rate is a sign of misclassification.
Deflection rate
— target 60–80% for general support; lower is normal for technical or billing categories.
CSAT
— 1–5 star post-session survey. Compare agent CSAT vs human-handled CSAT to identify gaps.
Escalation rate by intent
— segment escalations by topic. A spike in a new category often signals a product issue or a missing KB article.
Hallucination spot-check

— sample 1–2% of deflected sessions and verify that every cited KB article actually supports the answer given. High hallucination rates mean the KB is out of date or the retrieval threshold is too low.
Mean turns to resolution
— more than 4–5 turns before resolution suggests the agent is asking unnecessary clarifying questions or failing to find the right KB content.
PII Handling in Support Conversations

Users often share sensitive data (order numbers, account emails, sometimes credit card fragments) in chat. Two rules: never log raw user messages to a third-party LLM without a data processing agreement (check your model provider's terms for support use cases), and configure the system prompt to prohibit the agent from echoing back PII. For regulated industries (healthcare, finance), consider running a PII redaction pass on user messages before they enter the LLM context, storing the original separately for human-agent reference only.
